title: Introduction to Probability вҖ” Practice Questions

questions.title: Conditional probability definition
questions.prompt: Define conditional probability P(A | B). State Bayes' theorem. What does it mean for two events A and B to be independent?

markscheme.text: P(A | B) = P(A вҲ© B) / P(B), defined when P(B) > 0
Bayes' theorem: P(A | B) = P(B | A) В· P(A) / P(B)
A and B are independent if P(A вҲ© B) = P(A) В· P(B), equivalently P(A | B) = P(A) (knowledge of B gives no information about A)

questions.title: Law of total probability
questions.prompt: State the Law of Total Probability. Explain when it is useful and give a medical testing example: a disease affects 1% of the population. A test is 90% sensitive (P(positive | disease) = 0.9) and 95% specific (P(negative | no disease) = 0.95). Calculate the probability that a randomly chosen person tests positive.

markscheme.text: Law of total probability: if BвӮҒ,...,BвӮҷ partition the sample space, then P(A) = ОЈ P(A | Bбөў)В·P(Bбөў)
P(positive) = P(positive | disease)В·P(disease) + P(positive | no disease)В·P(no disease)

questions.title: Applying Bayes' theorem
questions.prompt: Using the medical testing scenario from above (disease prevalence 1%, sensitivity 90%, specificity 95%), calculate the probability that a person has the disease given that they tested positive. Comment on this result вҖ” why might it surprise a doctor?
questions.hint: Apply Bayes' theorem with the prior P(disease) = 0.01 and the likelihood P(positive | disease) = 0.9.

markscheme.text: P(disease | positive) = P(positive | disease) В· P(disease) / P(positive)
Numerator: 0.9 Г— 0.01 = 0.009
Denominator (from previous question): 0.0585
P(disease | positive) = 0.009 / 0.0585 вүҲ 0.154 (about 15%)

Interpretation: despite a 90% sensitive test, a positive result only means ~15% chance of having the disease. This is because the disease is rare (low prior), so most positives are false positives вҖ” known as the base rate fallacy

questions.title: Monty Hall and conditional independence
questions.prompt:
(i) In the Monty Hall problem, a contestant chooses door 1. The host (who knows where the car is) opens door 3 to reveal a goat. Should the contestant switch to door 2? Prove your answer using Bayes' theorem.
(ii) Define conditional independence: A and B are conditionally independent given C. Give an example where two events are conditionally independent given a third but not marginally independent.
questions.hint: For (i), let Cбөў be the event 'car is behind door i'. Compute P(CвӮҒ | host opens 3) and P(CвӮӮ | host opens 3).

markscheme.text: Let Cбөў = car behind door i, HвӮғ = host opens door 3. Priors: P(CвӮҒ)=P(CвӮӮ)=P(CвӮғ)=1/3
P(HвӮғ | CвӮҒ) = 1/2 (host can open 2 or 3); P(HвӮғ | CвӮӮ) = 1 (must open 3 as door 1 chosen and car at 2 вү  3); P(HвӮғ | CвӮғ) = 0
P(HвӮғ) = P(HвӮғ|CвӮҒ)В·1/3 + P(HвӮғ|CвӮӮ)В·1/3 + 0 = 1/6 + 1/3 = 1/2
Conditional independence: A вҠҘ B | C iff P(A вҲ© B | C) = P(A|C)В·P(B|C)

Example: let C = 'it is raining'; A = 'road is wet'; B = 'umbrella is used'. A and B are correlated (both caused by rain), so not marginally independent. But given C, knowing A gives no additional info about B (they share only the common cause C) вҖ” conditionally independent given C

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: You have two coins: coin A is fair (P(heads)=0.5) and coin B is biased (P(heads)=0.8). You pick a coin at random (each with probability 0.5) and flip it twice, obtaining HH. Calculate the posterior probability that you chose coin A.

markscheme.text: Prior: P(A)=P(B)=0.5. Likelihood: P(HH|A)=0.25, P(HH|B)=0.64

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Using P(A|HH) вүҲ 0.281 as your new prior, you flip the same coin again and observe T. Update the posterior to find P(A | HHT). Show your work.

markscheme.text: New prior: P(A)=0.281, P(B)=0.719. Likelihood: P(T|A)=0.5, P(T|B)=0.2

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Explain the concepts of prior, likelihood, posterior, and evidence in Bayesian inference. How does the posterior become the prior for the next observation (sequential updating)?

markscheme.text: Prior P(H): your belief about hypothesis H before seeing data
Likelihood P(D|H): how probable the observed data D is under hypothesis H
Evidence P(D): total probability of data under all hypotheses (normalising constant)
Posterior P(H|D) вҲқ P(D|H)В·P(H). For sequential data: posterior after observing DвӮҒ becomes the prior for DвӮӮ; equivalent to processing all data at once (multiplication of likelihoods)

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Three machines A, B, C produce 20%, 30%, 50% of a factory's output respectively. The defect rates are 5%, 3%, 2%. A defective item is found. What is the probability it came from machine A?

markscheme.text: P(A | defective) = P(defective|A)В·P(A)/P(defective) = 0.01/0.029

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe the naГҜve Bayes classifier for spam filtering. What is the 'naГҜve' assumption, and when is it reasonable?

markscheme.text: Model: classify email as spam/ham based on word frequencies. P(spam|words) вҲқ P(spam)В·вҲҸ P(wбөў|spam)
NaГҜve assumption: words are conditionally independent given the class label (given spam, occurrence of 'free' doesn't affect probability of 'win')
Reasonable when: dependencies between words are weak or cancel out in practice; often works well empirically despite the assumption being false

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: You have a training set where 40% of emails are spam. In spam emails, 'free' appears in 60% and 'meeting' in 5%. In ham, 'free' appears in 10% and 'meeting' in 40%. A new email contains both 'free' and 'meeting'. Classify it using naГҜve Bayes. Show all working.

markscheme.text: P(spam) = 0.4, P(ham) = 0.6
P(free, meeting | spam) = 0.6 Г— 0.05 = 0.03 (naГҜve assumption); P(free, meeting | ham) = 0.1 Г— 0.4 = 0.04
Score(ham) > Score(spam) вҶ’ classify as ham. P(spam | email) = 0.012/(0.012+0.024) = 1/3 вүҲ 33%

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: What problem arises in naГҜve Bayes if a word never appears in the training set for one class? Describe the Laplace (add-one) smoothing solution.

markscheme.text: Problem: if P(w|spam) = 0 (word never seen in spam training), the entire product becomes 0 вҖ” the classifier ignores all other evidence
Laplace smoothing: add a pseudo-count of 1 to every word count. P(w|class) = (count(w, class) + 1) / (total words in class + |vocabulary|)
Effect: no probability is zero; rare words get a small positive probability; classifier remains robust to unseen words

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: In practice, naГҜve Bayes multiplies many small probabilities, causing numerical underflow. How is this addressed?

markscheme.text: Work in log-space: compute log P(class) + ОЈ log P(wбөў|class). Compare log-scores rather than probabilities
Avoids multiplying many small numbers; additions are numerically stable; final classification based on which class has the higher log-score

questions.title: Discrete random variable basics
questions.prompt: Define: (i) a discrete random variable X; (ii) its probability mass function (PMF); (iii) the expected value E[X]. State the condition that a PMF must satisfy.

markscheme.text: Discrete RV: a function X: О© вҶ’ в„қ where О© is a sample space; X takes at most countably many values
PMF: p(x) = P(X = x) for each value x. Conditions: p(x) вүҘ 0 for all x, and ОЈ_x p(x) = 1
Expected value: E[X] = ОЈ_x x В· p(x) (sum over all values weighted by probability)

questions.title: Geometric distribution and memorylessness
questions.prompt: The geometric distribution models the number of trials until the first success (with success probability p). State its PMF, expected value, and the memoryless property. Verify the memoryless property formally.

markscheme.text: PMF: P(X = k) = (1вҲ’p)^(kвҲ’1) В· p for k = 1, 2, 3, ... (number of trials until first success)
Memoryless property: P(X > m+n | X > m) = P(X > n) вҖ” given that the first success hasn't occurred in m trials, the distribution of remaining trials is the same as the original
Verification: P(X > m) = (1вҲ’p)^m. P(X > m+n | X > m) = P(X > m+n)/P(X > m) = (1вҲ’p)^(m+n)/(1вҲ’p)^m = (1вҲ’p)^n = P(X > n) вң“

questions.title: Expectation by indicator variables
questions.prompt: A fair coin is flipped n times. Let X be the number of heads. Using indicator random variables, derive E[X]. Then generalise: a biased coin with P(heads) = p is flipped n times. What is E[X]? Now, in a group of 30 people, what is the expected number of pairs who share a birthday? (Assume 365 days in a year.)
questions.hint: For the birthday problem, define an indicator variable for each pair. There are C(30,2) pairs.

markscheme.text: Let Iбөў = 1 if flip i is heads. E[Iбөў] = P(heads) = 1/2. X = IвӮҒ+...+IвӮҷ; E[X] = nВ·(1/2) = n/2 by linearity of expectation
Biased coin: E[X] = nВ·p (same argument)
Birthday problem: define Iбөўвұј = 1 if persons i and j share a birthday. P(same birthday) = 1/365
Number of pairs = C(30,2) = 435; E[shared birthday pairs] = 435 Г— 1/365 вүҲ 1.19

questions.title: Functions of random variables and LOTUS
questions.prompt:
(i) State the Law of the Unconscious Statistician (LOTUS) for discrete random variables.
(ii) A fair die has faces {1,2,3,4,5,6}. Let X be the result of one roll. Find E[X], E[XВІ], and E[(XвҲ’E[X])ВІ] (the variance).
(iii) You win ВЈXВІ for rolling X. Is this a fair game at a cost of ВЈ15 per roll? Justify.
questions.hint: Use LOTUS for E[XВІ]: E[g(X)] = ОЈ g(x)В·P(X=x).

markscheme.text: LOTUS: for g: в„қвҶ’в„қ, E[g(X)] = ОЈ_x g(x)В·P(X=x)
Expected winnings per roll = E[XВІ] = 91/6 вүҲ 15.17 > 15. Yes, slightly favourable at ВЈ15 cost (expected profit вүҲ 17p per roll)

questions.title: Coupon collector and expected waiting times
questions.prompt: This question concerns the Coupon Collector Problem.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: There are n types of coupon; each purchase gives a uniformly random coupon type. Let T be the total number of purchases to collect all n types. Express T = TвӮҒ + TвӮӮ + ... + TвӮҷ where Tбөў is the number of purchases to get the i-th new type after already having iвҲ’1 distinct types. State the distribution of Tбөў and find E[Tбөў].

markscheme.text: After iвҲ’1 distinct coupons, probability of getting a new one is p = (nвҲ’i+1)/n. Each trial is independent.
Tбөў ~ Geometric((nвҲ’i+1)/n)
E[Tбөў] = 1/p = n/(nвҲ’i+1)

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Using part (a) and linearity of expectation, derive E[T] in terms of n and the harmonic number HвӮҷ = 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + ... + 1/n. Evaluate for n=4.

markscheme.text: E[T] = ОЈбөўвӮҢвӮҒвҒҝ E[Tбөў] = ОЈбөўвӮҢвӮҒвҒҝ n/(nвҲ’i+1) = nВ·ОЈвұјвӮҢвӮҒвҒҝ 1/j = nВ·HвӮҷ (substituting j = nвҲ’i+1)

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Since HвӮҷ вүҲ ln n for large n, E[T] вүҲ n ln n. Explain intuitively why the last few coupons take so long to collect. Compare this to the O(n log n) complexity of comparison sorting вҖ” is the connection a coincidence?

markscheme.text: The last coupon: probability of getting the final missing type is 1/n, so expected wait is n trials. The last few distinct types take O(n) each вҶ’ they dominate the sum

E[T] = nВ·HвӮҷ вүҲ n ln n. The O(n log n) lower bound for comparison sorting also arises from a counting argument (n! permutations вҶ’ logвӮӮ(n!) вүҲ n logвӮӮ n). Both are instances of the same information-theoretic structure
Not a coincidence: both problems require 'distinguishing' n objects; the total number of bits of information needed is Оҳ(n log n) in both cases

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: Let X be the number of coupons until the first duplicate. Show that E[X] = Оҳ(вҲҡn). (Hint: use the birthday paradox вҖ” after k draws, what is the probability of no duplicate?)

markscheme.text: P(no duplicate in k draws) = 1 Г— (nвҲ’1)/n Г— (nвҲ’2)/n Г— ... Г— (nвҲ’k+1)/n = О _{i=0}^{k-1}(1 вҲ’ i/n)
Using 1вҲ’x вүҲ e^(вҲ’x): product вүҲ e^(вҲ’k(kвҲ’1)/(2n)). This вүҲ 1/2 when k(kвҲ’1)/2 вүҲ nВ·ln 2, i.e. k вүҲ вҲҡ(2nВ·ln 2) = Оҳ(вҲҡn)
E[X] вүҲ ОЈ_{k=0}^{вҲһ} P(no duplicate in k draws) вүҲ ОЈ e^{вҲ’kВІ/(2n)} вүҲ вҲҡ(ПҖn/2) = Оҳ(вҲҡn)

questions.title: Binomial distribution and applications
questions.prompt: This question concerns the Binomial distribution.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Define the Binomial distribution B(n, p). Derive its PMF and state its expected value and variance.

markscheme.text: B(n,p): number of successes in n independent Bernoulli(p) trials. PMF: P(X=k) = C(n,k)В·p^kВ·(1вҲ’p)^(nвҲ’k) for k=0,...,n
Derivation: C(n,k) ways to choose which k trials succeed; each such outcome has probability p^kВ·(1вҲ’p)^(nвҲ’k)
E[X] = np; Var(X) = np(1вҲ’p) (both derived by indicator variable decomposition X = ОЈ Iбөў)

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: A network packet is independently dropped with probability p = 0.01 at each of n = 10 routers. Let X be the number of drops. Find P(X вүҘ 2). Would you expect the Poisson approximation to be accurate here? Justify.

markscheme.text: P(XвүҘ2) вүҲ 1 вҲ’ 0.9044 вҲ’ 0.0914 = 0.0042. Poisson(О»=0.1) would give P(XвүҘ2)=1вҲ’e^{вҲ’0.1}вҲ’0.1e^{вҲ’0.1}вүҲ0.0047; reasonable approximation (n large, p small, О»=np=0.1 moderate)

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: State the Poisson distribution Poisson(О»). Prove that it arises as the limit of B(n, О»/n) as nвҶ’вҲһ with О» fixed.

markscheme.text: Poisson(О»): P(X=k) = e^{вҲ’О»}В·О»^k/k! for k=0,1,2,...; E[X]=Var(X)=О»
Starting from B(n, О»/n): P(X=k) = C(n,k)В·(О»/n)^kВ·(1вҲ’О»/n)^{nвҲ’k}
C(n,k)/n^k = n(nвҲ’1)В·В·В·(nвҲ’k+1)/n^k вҶ’ 1 as nвҶ’вҲһ (k fixed). (1вҲ’О»/n)^n вҶ’ e^{вҲ’О»}. (1вҲ’О»/n)^{вҲ’k} вҶ’ 1
So P(X=k) вҶ’ (1В·О»^k/k!)В·e^{вҲ’О»}В·1 = e^{вҲ’О»}О»^k/k! as required

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: The sum of independent Poisson random variables is Poisson. If X ~ Poisson(О»вӮҒ) and Y ~ Poisson(О»вӮӮ) are independent, state the distribution of X + Y and compute E[X+Y] and Var(X+Y).

markscheme.text: X+Y ~ Poisson(О»вӮҒ+О»вӮӮ)
E[X+Y] = E[X]+E[Y] = О»вӮҒ+О»вӮӮ (linearity of expectation)
Var(X+Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y) = О»вӮҒ+О»вӮӮ (since X and Y are independent)

questions.topicTitle: Expectation Properties, Variance, and Discrete Distributions
questions.title: Variance and standard deviation
questions.prompt: Define the variance Var(X) of a random variable X. State the alternative formula Var(X) = E[XВІ] вҲ’ (E[X])ВІ. What is the standard deviation? For a Bernoulli(p) random variable, compute E[X], E[XВІ], and Var(X).

markscheme.text: Var(X) = E[(X вҲ’ E[X])ВІ]; standard deviation = вҲҡVar(X). Alternative: Var(X) = E[XВІ] вҲ’ (E[X])ВІ
Bernoulli(p): P(X=1)=p, P(X=0)=1вҲ’p. E[X] = p
E[XВІ] = 1ВІВ·p + 0ВІВ·(1вҲ’p) = p. Var(X) = p вҲ’ pВІ = p(1вҲ’p)

questions.topicTitle: Expectation Properties, Variance, and Discrete Distributions
questions.title: Linearity of expectation and variance rules
questions.prompt: State: (i) linearity of expectation; (ii) the formula for Var(aX + b); (iii) the formula for Var(X + Y) when X and Y are independent. Prove (iii).

markscheme.text: Linearity: E[aX+b] = aВ·E[X]+b; E[X+Y] = E[X]+E[Y] (always, no independence needed)
Var(aX+b) = aВІВ·Var(X) (constants shift mean but don't change spread; squaring makes scale factor aВІ)
Var(X+Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y) when independent (Cov(X,Y)=0)
Proof: Var(X+Y) = E[(X+Y)ВІ]вҲ’(E[X+Y])ВІ = E[XВІ]+2E[XY]+E[YВІ]вҲ’(E[X])ВІвҲ’2E[X]E[Y]вҲ’(E[Y])ВІ. Since independent: E[XY]=E[X]E[Y], so 2E[XY]вҲ’2E[X]E[Y]=0. Remaining: Var(X)+Var(Y)

questions.topicTitle: Expectation Properties, Variance, and Discrete Distributions
questions.title: Markov and Chebyshev inequalities
questions.prompt:
(i) State Markov's inequality and Chebyshev's inequality.
(ii) A random variable X has E[X]=50 and Var(X)=25. Use Chebyshev's inequality to bound P(|XвҲ’50|вүҘ15).
(iii) Compare this bound to the exact value if X ~ Binomial(100, 0.5). Is Chebyshev tight here?
questions.hint: Chebyshev: P(|XвҲ’Ој|вүҘk) вүӨ Var(X)/kВІ for any k>0.

markscheme.text: Markov: for non-negative X, P(XвүҘa) вүӨ E[X]/a. Chebyshev: P(|XвҲ’Ој|вүҘk) вүӨ ПғВІ/kВІ where ПғВІ=Var(X)
X~B(100,0.5): E[X]=50, Var(X)=25. P(|XвҲ’50|вүҘ15) = P(XвүӨ35 or XвүҘ65) is very small (вүҲ0.003 by normal approximation or exact binomial вҖ” much smaller than 1/9)
Chebyshev is very loose here; it's distribution-agnostic so gives a weak bound. For specific distributions, tighter bounds are available

questions.topicTitle: Expectation Properties, Variance, and Discrete Distributions
questions.title: Generating functions
questions.prompt:
(i) Define the probability generating function (PGF) G_X(z) of a discrete non-negative integer-valued RV X.
(ii) Show how E[X] and Var(X) can be extracted from G_X.
(iii) Find the PGF of a Poisson(О») random variable and use it to compute E[X] and Var(X).
questions.hint: Differentiate the PGF at z=1. For Var(X), use G''(1)+G'(1)вҲ’(G'(1))ВІ.

markscheme.text: PGF: G_X(z) = E[z^X] = ОЈ_{k=0}^вҲһ P(X=k)В·z^k
E[X] = G'_X(1) (differentiate and set z=1); E[X(XвҲ’1)] = G''_X(1); Var(X) = G''(1)+G'(1)вҲ’(G'(1))ВІ
Poisson(О»): G_X(z) = ОЈ e^{вҲ’О»}О»^k z^k/k! = e^{вҲ’О»}В·e^{О»z} = e^{О»(zвҲ’1)}
G''(z) = О»ВІe^{О»(zвҲ’1)}; G''(1) = О»ВІ; Var(X) = О»ВІ+О»вҲ’О»ВІ = О» вң“

questions.topicTitle: Expectation Properties, Variance, and Discrete Distributions
questions.title: Central Limit Theorem and random walks
questions.prompt: This question concerns sums of random variables.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: State the Central Limit Theorem (CLT). A fair coin is flipped n=900 times; let X be the number of heads. Use the CLT to approximate P(435 вүӨ X вүӨ 465).

markscheme.text: CLT: if XвӮҒ,...,XвӮҷ are i.i.d. with mean Ој and variance ПғВІ, then (XМ„ вҲ’ Ој)/(Пғ/вҲҡn) вҶ’ N(0,1) in distribution as nвҶ’вҲһ; equivalently SвӮҷ = ОЈ Xбөў вүҲ N(nОј, nПғВІ)
X~B(900,0.5): E[X]=450, Var(X)=225, Пғ=15. Standardise: P(435вүӨXвүӨ465) вүҲ P((435вҲ’450)/15 вүӨ Z вүӨ (465вҲ’450)/15) = P(вҲ’1вүӨZвүӨ1)
P(вҲ’1вүӨZвүӨ1) вүҲ 0.683 (68.3% rule for normal distribution)

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: A symmetric random walk on в„Ө starts at 0. At each step, move +1 with probability 1/2 and вҲ’1 with probability 1/2. Let SвӮҷ be the position after n steps. Find E[SвӮҷ] and Var(SвӮҷ).

markscheme.text: Write SвӮҷ = XвӮҒ+...+XвӮҷ where Xбөў = Вұ1 with probability 1/2 each. E[Xбөў]=0, Var(Xбөў)=1
E[SвӮҷ] = nВ·E[XвӮҒ] = 0 (by linearity)
Var(SвӮҷ) = nВ·Var(XвӮҒ) = n (by independence). So std dev = вҲҡn вҖ” random walk spreads as вҲҡn

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Explain what 'recurrence' means for a random walk. State whether the symmetric 1D random walk is recurrent. How does this change in 2D and 3D?

markscheme.text: Recurrent: the walk returns to its starting position with probability 1 (infinitely often)
1D symmetric random walk: recurrent вҖ” by the CLT, the walk visits every integer infinitely often almost surely
2D: also recurrent (PГіlya 1921 вҖ” probability of return to origin is 1)
3D: transient вҖ” probability of return to origin < 1 (вүҲ0.34). The phrase: 'a drunk man will find his way home, but a drunk bird may get lost forever'

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: State Jensen's inequality. Use it to show that E[вҲҡX] вүӨ вҲҡE[X] for any non-negative random variable X. Give a concrete numerical example.

markscheme.text: Jensen's inequality: for a convex function f, E[f(X)] вүҘ f(E[X]). For concave f, the inequality reverses
f(x) = вҲҡx is concave (f''(x) < 0). By Jensen: E[вҲҡX] вүӨ вҲҡ(E[X])
Example: X takes values 1 and 9 each with prob 1/2. E[X]=5. E[вҲҡX]=(1+3)/2=2. вҲҡ(E[X])=вҲҡ5вүҲ2.24. Indeed 2 вүӨ 2.24 вң“

questions.topicTitle: Expectation Properties, Variance, and Discrete Distributions
questions.title: Negative binomial and conditional expectation
questions.prompt: This question concerns counting distributions and conditional expectation.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Define the Negative Binomial distribution NB(r, p). State its PMF, mean, and variance. Explain the relationship between NB(1, p) and the Geometric distribution.

markscheme.text: NB(r,p): number of failures before the r-th success in a sequence of Bernoulli(p) trials. PMF: P(X=k) = C(k+rвҲ’1, k)В·(1вҲ’p)^kВ·p^r for k=0,1,2,...
E[X] = r(1вҲ’p)/p; Var(X) = r(1вҲ’p)/pВІ
NB(1,p): number of failures before first success = Geometric(p) вҲ’ 1 (where Geometric counts total trials). Same memoryless structure

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Define conditional expectation E[X|Y=y]. State the tower property (law of total expectation). Use it to find E[X] where X|N ~ Binomial(N, p) and N ~ Poisson(О»).

markscheme.text: E[X|Y=y] = ОЈ_x xВ·P(X=x|Y=y); tower property: E[X] = E[E[X|Y]] = ОЈ_y E[X|Y=y]В·P(Y=y)
E[X|N] = NВ·p (mean of Binomial(N,p))
E[X] = E[E[X|N]] = E[NВ·p] = pВ·E[N] = pО»

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Find Var(X) for the same compound distribution X|N~Bin(N,p), N~Poisson(О»). Use the law of total variance: Var(X) = E[Var(X|N)] + Var(E[X|N]).

markscheme.text: Var(X|N) = NВ·p(1вҲ’p); E[Var(X|N)] = E[N]В·p(1вҲ’p) = О»p(1вҲ’p)
E[X|N] = Np; Var(E[X|N]) = Var(Np) = pВІВ·Var(N) = pВІВ·О»
Var(X) = О»p(1вҲ’p) + О»pВІ = О»p(1вҲ’p+p) = О»p. (Note: X ~ Poisson(О»p) by compound Poisson result)

parts.label: d
parts.prompt: State the weak law of large numbers. Prove it using Chebyshev's inequality.

markscheme.text: WLLN: if XвӮҒ,...,XвӮҷ are i.i.d. with mean Ој and finite variance ПғВІ, then for any Оө>0: P(|XМ„вӮҷ вҲ’ Ој| вүҘ Оө) вҶ’ 0 as nвҶ’вҲһ
Proof: E[XМ„вӮҷ] = Ој; Var(XМ„вӮҷ) = ПғВІ/n
By Chebyshev: P(|XМ„вӮҷвҲ’Ој|вүҘОө) вүӨ Var(XМ„вӮҷ)/ОөВІ = ПғВІ/(nОөВІ) вҶ’ 0 as nвҶ’вҲһ вң“
